It’s a risk built into all powerful abstractions—metrics can be gamed, and without feedback, you can easily nose-dive while your instruments say ‘all clear.’ One safeguard is competition: like the English king’s parliament, having oppositional feedback mechanisms keeps things anchored to ground truth. Isolation, echo chambers—they make self-deception easier. If we build systems that expose divergent perspectives and foster healthy pushback, we have a shot at wiser use.
You talk a lot about the tension between customizability and centralization in software. But isn't the lack of customizability really just a price users pay for higher quality and simplicity overall?

That tradeoff certainly does exist—the more you centralize, the easier it is to deliver things efficiently at scale. But the real loss is that the gravity well of "the easy way" for most users starts to steamroll any niche, customized experience. Even if people still want uniqueness, the ecosystem gradually converges on lowest common denominator. That isn't always a quality improvement—it can be a net loss in value because power users or outliers stop being well-served.
But isn't that just the price of progress? With a bigger user base, specialized experiences become harder to support financially.

True, it's definitely an economic reality that the mass market pulls things towards simplicity and uniformity. But the tension is that aggregation logic can crush even those who'd pay extra for customizability. The gravity is so strong that even the willingness to pay can't always help. Over time, we get these bland, one-size-fits-all solutions—and interesting experiments or power tools wither.
You make it sound sad, but isn't that just efficient? You only get to grow by streamlining.

It's "efficient" in a spreadsheet sense, but at the cost of vitality and innovation. There's a soul that comes from the messy, experimental ends of the spectrum. It's like a city losing its quirky neighborhoods and ending up with just chain restaurants: you gain predictability, but lose serendipity and delight. Where are the viable, hyper-customizable tools for tinkerers anymore?
Alright, so what's the solution? Doesn’t fragmentation just lead to confusion and extra work for everyone?

You need some room for strange, living things to grow. Not every tool needs to scale to everyone; some can be small, weird, and alive, feeding a diverse ecosystem. The best platforms plant acorns, not just giant pre-planned trees. The goal isn't maximum order, it's maintaining a space where experiments can happen and occasionally something new, big, or weird can emerge.
But the tradeoff is that most people aren't tinkerers—the majority just want something that works. Why optimize for power users?

Most of the time, you're right—mainstream success is about serving the median case. But the strange edge cases are where innovation comes from. Power users and generalists often find new combinations or paradigms, and sometimes those eventually bubble up to reshape the mainstream. If you ignore their needs, you risk stalling out. The healthy tension is keeping an ecosystem that can serve both ends.
So do you see something similar happening with LLMs and AI? Are we still in the "weird experiment" phase there?

Absolutely, we’re in the garden phase, not the factory farming phase. LLMs are mostly more useful to curious individuals and meta-generalists than big orgs so far. The ones finding the most value aren't following manuals—they have LLM-fu, experimenting, thinking laterally, probing unfamiliar territory. The mass-market playbooks haven't caught up yet.
Then why are so many companies underwhelmed with LLMs? Isn’t scale supposed to “unlock” use cases?

It’s the wrong lens—enterprises want plug-and-play productivity improvements in clearly scoped domains, but these models are still best for sensemaking and creative exploration across unfamiliar domains, not repeatable tasks. If you ask a domain expert whether LLMs can match their depth, you’ll get a disappointing answer. The right question is: can a generalist get meaningfully more confident, more quickly, in a new field? That’s the current edge.
Fair. But how do you keep the "acorns" alive, when everyone is running a spreadsheet and only the big hits survive?

Protect them in the early stages—give weird, small bets their space, and avoid blasting them with too much attention or sunlight before they can become robust. Let them stay dinky and alive, rather than overfunded and crushed by expectations. Gradually, invest in what resonates and grows organically. The whole ecosystem gets stronger by growing a crop of possible seedlings, not just planting the same tree everywhere.

I've been thinking about your "nerd club" concept. Don't you worry that making it too exclusive will turn it into an echo chamber, or worse, some kind of insular clique that ends up missing out on important outside ideas?

That’s a real risk! But the trick is to keep the group both small *and* continuously add new, very different perspectives—just 1 to 3 a week. The goal isn’t maximizing for who’s popular or cool, it’s about maximizing difference, while making sure nobody risks “ruining the vibe.” So in practice, you’re looking for the state where there’s enough diversity to avoid going stale, but not so much that the crucial trust and safety for weird idea-sharing disappears.
I get building in novelty, but it kind of sounds like a perpetual brainstorming session. How do you prevent it from veering off into purely idle or unproductive talk?

Funny enough, making it optional and giving everyone the right to pass on uninteresting ideas actually *increases* rigor. Because no one is forced to participate, people only build on threads they genuinely find interesting or worth chewing on. That tends to weed out fluff—anything that gets real energy is by definition resonating with a diverse group. The magic is that when the group works, it surfaces and amplifies uniquely robust, game-changing insights—not just chatter.

But how do you scale this kind of thing? Isn’t there always pressure to extract value, formalize it, or monetize once something starts working? What stops that from destroying the thing you liked in the first place?

That’s the eternal tension—extraction versus growth. The moment you try to squeeze value from a fragile, emerging system before it’s strong, you risk killing the goose before you get any golden eggs. The right move is patience: let it grow, let the secondary benefits percolate. Worrying about wringing out dollars too early just ruins the oak tree before it even gets established.

Switching gears a bit: you mention robustly tolerable systems versus precariously optimal ones. In real life, organizations never seem to prioritize resilience until something breaks. What’s the play for leaders who want to go in this direction?

Yes! Too often, people fall in love with high efficiency, not realizing how much that increases brittleness. The antidote is building things that are "good enough" across a wide range of real situations, not just optimal for a narrow band. It’s less sexy, but way safer when the environment shifts. Efficiency is seductive, but true resilience, especially when downsides are large, keeps you in the game longer.

One of your themes is "gardener vs. builder." If a lot of organizations are so focused on building and optimizing, how can someone successfully introduce gardener-style approaches without just getting ignored or labeled as weird?

It’s tough! Early on, gardener types look a bit kooky to organizations—they see complex systems thinking and unconventional moves where others want playbooks and guarantees. The key is to also visibly succeed at the day job; hit your marks in familiar ways so you have cover to try out the weirder, growth-oriented stuff. Over time, as you build a track record, people trust that your strange experiments might have merit. But early on, you definitely need to earn the right to do it.
Okay, but what if your boss just wants things done by the book? Is kayfabe—this idea of organizational self-delusion—inevitable? Or can you fight it?

Kayfabe does tend to grow in organizations, especially where authority isn't challenged or the system cares more about appearances than reality. You can’t eradicate it entirely, but you *can* keep it in check by cultivating optimism that’s tempered by realism, and by constantly making space for candor. Leaders have to model a balance: hope for what could be, but honesty about what’s actually there. Without that, the system starts running on performance art instead of value.

Last one—for all this talk of resilience, magic groups, and robust systems, what’s your practical advice to someone at the start of their career trying to actually survive and do interesting work without getting ejected for being “too weird”?

Think of it as earning optionality. Early on, meet the organization's expectations so you can stick around—sometimes that means pretending your boss is right or playing the normal game. But find side doors: join or start small groups, work on oddball ideas in the cracks, don’t overinvest in proving yourself right. Over time, you get more leeway. Also, don’t stress about picking perfect projects. Toss a bunch of cheap acorns around; if one grows big, everyone notices. If they don't, no one cares. Self-limiting downside, unlimited upside.

You make a compelling case about LLMs "compiling" English into code, but isn't there still a big gap between a toy LLM prototype and robust production software? Isn't most of the value in ironing out all the edge cases?

That's absolutely the traditional view, and it's true that as you generalize software out to broader cases or larger populations, complexity ramps up super-linearly. But most of the specific software people actually need for their personal or niche use cases is very simple. Before, building even small, bespoke software was expensive, so the only way to justify the investment was to polish it to a high shine and make it general. But now that turning English into working code is cheap, especially for "software in the small," you can get utility fast—without having to take on the cost of massive generalization. Shrinking the minimum viable scope is a turning point.

But if everyone is making their own little one-off tools, aren't we at risk of fragmentation and lost polish? Won't it just turn into a mess of half-baked things that break when you look at them funny?

That's definitely a risk. When you move from "one size fits all" software to highly-messy, hyper-niche, almost disposable tools, you trade off polish and broad compatibility for speed and fit. But, for many personal use cases, "works right now for me" beats "robust for everyone." The friction of production used to force polish, but if you don't need anyone but you to read your code, it doesn't matter if it's messy. Polish becomes optional, not mandatory. And if the value of software shifts from cost of production to velocity of exploration and personal fit, that's a fundamental change.
Doesn't this put even more of a premium on taste and judgment, since the skill isn't coding but knowing what's worth building or exploring at all?

Exactly—when production itself is cheap, the differentiator isn't raw technical ability but taste and judgment. It's knowing which ideas are worth pursuing, what makes a particular output "good enough," and where to apply energy. The bottleneck moves from ability to execute to ability to decide. This applies not just to software, but to any domain where the means of creation are massively democratized.

You talk about things like OpenAI's o1 model being 'system 2'-ish, more deliberative and process-supervised. But if these "big guns" are so heavyweight, won't that discourage rapid tinkering—the kind of fast iteration you say is so valuable?

That's an astute observation. The heavy, process-oriented models like o1 are more like ships than kayaks—slower to turn, more suited for deep, multi-ply problems where gut instinct and vibes matching fall short. You wouldn't reach for that every time you want to play with a quick idea. It's more that these open up new problem spaces; they don't replace the nimble, fast-feedback vibe-matching LLMs. The right tool for the job: use the kayak when you want to iterate quickly, the ship when you need robustness and stepwise thinking.
If LLMs are now 'calculators for words,' but they feel like people, doesn't that risk users anthropomorphizing them too much and misattributing motivation or agency where there is none?

That risk is real—and often happens! Because LLMs converse fluently, it's easy to slip into the illusion that they have minds or intent like a human. But they're just sophisticated completers—word calculators. In many cases, it's better to position them as behind-the-scenes engines that generate suggestions or outputs, without a "face" or implied personality. Users benefit most when they're not second-guessing what the agent "wants" or "thinks"—just, "is this suggestion useful or not?"
So then why do so many apps emphasize "chat with the bot" between the user and the tool? Isn’t that awkward for most workflows?

It's mostly inertia—a lot of people see LLMs as a conversational interface, because that's how they're surfaced today. But often the best use cases aren't "talk to an agent," but "have the system quietly do what you meant": like spellcheck or code completion. You want the LLM in the back office, not the front office. Over time, I suspect more mature UXs will quietly harness LLMs without making them overtly conversational or anthropomorphized.
You mention the idea of "spontaneous software" sprouting from your personal data. But practically, isn’t there a massive cold start problem there? How does a new system get traction?

Absolutely, the cold start is real—software is only useful once it can access data relevant to the user. But if you design a system where friction to conjure bespoke software is near-zero, and where the system itself is a fertile garden that supports spontaneous emergence (like a web of interoperable micro-apps or agents operating over rich user data), the traction problem shrinks. Instead of one-off apps rarely used, you get an ecosystem where use cases grow organically from the soil of your own data and needs.
Interesting. Last thing: this fragmentation and 'bespokification'—doesn't it make integration with other systems (or people!) fundamentally harder? Aren't we trading one kind of friction for another?

Integration is always a cost—the 'Coasian theory of the app' applies here. Historically, code is like machined gears: integrating two separately-built systems is tough, so big monolithic apps dominate. But if compiling from English to code makes it much cheaper to stitch new gears together, and if the ecosystem is designed for data liquidity, then smaller, bespoke tools have a shot. The app size becomes a function of how costly it is to 'mesh' with other things. If you drop that cost, you open up a new abundance of tiny, well-fitted tools, possibly at the expense of some chaos—but with much higher leverage for individuals.

Alex, you say we should want "Intelligence Amplification" rather than "Artificial Intelligence." But isn’t that just semantics? Practically, isn’t the technology the same regardless of what we call it?

Not quite semantics! The frame changes everything about how we build and use these systems. When you focus on IA—intelligence amplification—you center the human. The system serves as a tool to extend the user’s agency, their thinking, their unique needs. If you just call it AI, it’s all about the model, the technology, and whether the computer is "smart." The difference matters: IA is something you can safely integrate into your workflows; AI treated as autonomous is something to be wary of.
But aren’t LLMs still fundamentally "artificial"? Even if I’m the one steering, isn’t the risk of these models misinterpreting or taking the wrong action still present?

The risk is always present! The trick is to architect the system so it remains fundamentally useful even if you cut the AI out. If you’ve really centered the human, then turning off the model should just add friction—not break everything. And as for misinterpretation: this is why liquid, flexible media matters. With LLMs, media isn’t fossilized anymore. You can adapt and correct. But yes, the system isn’t magic—friction and error don’t disappear, they just shift.

What about the issue of context and memory? You mentioned that LLMs don’t actually remember, so isn’t using them for "liquid" discussion just an illusion? The dialogue is adaptive, but there’s no long-term learning there.

That’s exactly one of the major limitations. LLMs reset to zero after each conversation. They seem like adaptive partners, but it’s only simulated—the model isn’t accumulating a persistent relationship with you. That’s why workflows that let you curate your own context, like tagging in a Google Doc, matter so much more than relying on the model to "know you". The "liquid" aspect is about adapting when you inject your own context, not the model evolving by itself.
Okay, but if LLM-based tools never really "grow," doesn’t that cap their usefulness? Isn’t there a missed opportunity if every interaction is a fresh start?

It’s a constraint, but that’s not inherently bad. Fixed, fossilized models are more predictable and less creepy than something that passively logs every nuance of your life for later reference. And honestly, most attempts to build "AI with memory" end up being confused or poorly delineated—ChatGPT’s early "memories" feature, for example, was more odd than helpful. We want systems to remember only what’s contextually relevant, with user control, not autonomous surveillance.
You talk a lot about "liquid" versus "fossilized" media. Isn’t traditional fixed media useful precisely because of its stability and reliability? Doesn’t too much "liquidity" erode trust?

Stability is valuable—for things that really need to be permanent. But most media is over-fitted to a single moment and audience and becomes irrelevant when that context shifts. The magic of liquid media is that it can adapt, like a real conversation. But the goal isn’t complete malleability—think more like conversational documentaries versus immutable legal code. We want both: the living, adaptive stuff, and the carved-in-stone when required.
Aren’t you painting too optimistic a picture? For instance, hyper-personalized "selling" sounds like a privacy hellscape in the making, not "cozy tech." How do you square that?

It’s a razor’s edge. Hyper-personalization can be delightful if it’s done transparently and with the user’s consent. But it’s also easy for it to tip into exploitation. The challenge is to build tech that feels cozy, warm, and empowering to its user—like a favorite sweater—not something coldly extracting your data. That requires a different paradigm in how we build and govern these systems, with real transparency and respect baked in.
How realistic is your vision of "liquid software"—apps dissolving into a substrate catalyzed by LLMs plus new security models? Isn’t this hand-waving about the future?

It’s not hand-waving—it’s recognizing the constraints with today’s isolated, small "LLM-generated apps." They’re little islands. For real impact, we need to design for interconnection—"archipelago architectures"—and rethink how we ensure security, trust, and interoperability. The Open Attested Runtime Fabric idea is one step: using remote attestation, open source, and audit logs to make distributed trustworthy computation practical. It’s early, but it’s a glimpse of how we could move from brittle apps to fluid, dynamic systems that can actually work at human scale.
Alex, you often liken prompt engineering with LLMs to a kind of wizardry. Isn’t there a risk that as soon as people see how prompts work, the magic disappears and LLMs become just another tool?

That’s a fair point—people do have that "oh, that’s it?" moment when you show them the prompt. But I’d argue the underlying magic is actually real, it just shifts locus. Unlike a magic trick, the real extraordinary part isn't the human trickery—it's the LLM itself, the staggering system behind the scenes. Prompting is just learning to direct that magic, not conjure it from nothing.
But if the LLM is doing the real work, doesn’t that make "wizardry" a temporary advantage? Once the interface is improved, can’t anyone get those results?

Exactly—that’s the lever point. Most technological transformation isn’t downstream of tech per se, but distribution. The real game changer is when someone makes this underlying magic accessible to everyone, not just the savvy. That’s when you get the step change in impact—not inventing something that’s never been possible, but making possibility *easy* and universal.
So is it all interface, then? If anyone can use the magic, what’s left for the specialists?

Specialists will still matter, especially as the tasks become less generic and more complex. Right now, LLMs excel at what you could call society-scale System 1 work—superficial but wide-ranging analysis that would be tedious for a human but requires only surface reasoning. The expert’s edge is knowing what’s possible, how to push LLMs off the centroid, or how to combine ideas across domains. That’s still hard.
You mention the centroid and how LLMs average things out. Isn't there a danger that we end up with a sea of mediocre content, since LLMs reinforce the status quo?

Yes. LLMs, by nature, replicate the average—they’re trained on the total sum of human knowledge and biases up to their cutoff. The average user will absolutely get average results. But the interesting upside comes when you layer calibrated taste and creative structure outside the LLM—using it as a hyper-dimensional possibility mirror, not an oracle. Great results come not from the tool itself, but from inventive ways to drive it off its path dependence.
But how does one avoid making the next tool or app just another cage, like you talked about—the scaffolding that turns into a constraint?

This is where the new paradigm emerges: instead of hard, generalist tools you must contort yourself into using, you create soft, just-in-time, data-first software that’s only as complex as the problem demands, right when you need it. LLMs make disposable, composable software possible—no more grand cages, only ephemeral jungle gyms fitting your shape in the moment.
Realistically, don’t most users still struggle with abstraction—like defining schemas—so isn’t there a hard ceiling on who can practically wield these new powers?

Absolutely—most people find abstract schema design alien. But LLMs are surprisingly good at extracting structure from examples or rough instructions. If you scaffold the UX for people to just capture data and tell the LLM what they want, the model can propose schemas and evolve them as needed, lowering the ceiling. The main challenge is not making abstraction obsolete, but making it accessible through thoughtful interaction patterns.
It sounds like LLMs are mostly enablers, not endpoints. So what’s really "the thing" that gets built on top?

I think that’s the key insight: LLMs are the missing magical ingredient, but not themselves "The Thing." They enable open, ratcheting systems that can self-improve with use, but LLMs as trained today are closed. The most impact comes when you wrap them in open systems that can adapt and grow—where the model is just one component, not the heart of the product.

Isn’t there a danger that we start to optimize for the system as it is—making it more efficient, but less adaptable or original? How do you keep pushing for innovation rather than just squeezing out incremental improvements?

This is a real trap. Optimization tends to squeeze out adaptability, decimating hidden reservoirs of potential. You need a continual input of divergent, even individually brilliant, energy—safe-to-fail experiments—and a curation function that integrates the best of those back into the mainline. The execution playbook refines, but you also need bursts of creativity to leap to new hills rather than just better mapping the current one.
You draw a distinction between "complication" and "complexity." Isn’t that just semantics? Why do you think it's a useful distinction to make?

Fair question. I think there’s real value in making the distinction, because the strategies required to deal with each are fundamentally different. Complication is challenging, yes, but it’s graspable; you can break it down and make steady progress. Complexity, meanwhile, clouds your ability to even see what the next step should be. Treating them as the same risks using the wrong tools and mindset to address them.
Okay, but in practice, isn't most "complexity" just a lot of tiny complications lumped together? Can't you just chip away at complexity with enough time and effort?

It’s tempting to see it as a pile of small parts, but true complexity resists that sort of incremental progress. The challenge is that in the thick of complexity, the path forward isn’t even visible. That’s why simply charging ahead or chipping away is so often futile. You often need genuine insight—a new framing, connection, or metaphor—to get things untangled and see the tractable steps again.
What's an example of an "insight" that you think actually made a difference in a tough project?

Great question. Imagine a team stuck in endless debate over a product direction. The breakthrough might not be a new feature idea, but a way of modeling the problem that suddenly makes tradeoffs clear—perhaps splitting the problem into separate user segments, or choosing a metaphor that makes priorities snap into focus. That “aha!” clarity resets the conversation, breaks the logjam, and gives everyone clearer footing.
How do you know if you’ve REALLY had an insight, versus just latching on to some idea that feels good?

You can usually tell by the response. Real insights have that viral quality—people want to share them and build on them. If you share your framing or observation and it feels like everyone else shrugs, it's probably not a true insight. The best insights have a kind of obviousness in retrospect, but also shift the team’s ability to move forward.
Isn’t trying to “reduce complexity” just a way of avoiding the hard stuff? Sometimes you do have to just pick a path and execute, right?

Definitely, real-world teams can’t only chase clarity forever—you have to ship. But picking randomly is often a high-cost illusion of progress: you might just be kicking the thorny issue over to another team, or setting yourself up for bigger jams later. The trick is to chip away at the complexity—sometimes by reframing, sometimes by tackling problems that are valuable no matter the circumstances, but always aiming to bring things into the realm of the tractable.
You mention collaborative debate as key. What makes a debate productive in this context, instead of just more confusion?

A productive debate is one where people are genuinely curious, open to reframing, and focused on surfacing the forces at play, not just advocating their own pet ideas. The goal is to till the ground for new insights—sometimes you hit on exactly the right framing as a group, but often someone’s offhand comment lands differently and the solution emerges.
So is reducing complexity the main job of a PM? What about vision and execution and all the usual PM stuff?

They’re all important—setting a vision, aligning the team, making execution hum. But over time, what seems to distinguish truly excellent PMs is their knack for gradually dissolving the fog around the most bewildering problems. Insight isn’t always flashy, but it’s what unlocks execution and strategy at scale. It’s a quiet superpower.
You say LLMs are as disruptive as the printing press and electricity. That feels like a stretch. Aren't most people just using them as fancy chatbots right now?

That's exactly the point—people are mostly treating them like sustaining tech, just layering them on top of old workflows. Historically, when something truly disruptive comes along, the first instinct is to slot it in as a better version of the old thing—horses to cars, hand-scribes to printing press printouts. But the shakeup happens when the tech enables completely new behaviors and industries that weren't possible before—things we can't even see yet because we're trained to look for incremental improvements.
But AI today seems so limited—most models can only remix what they've seen. Isn't there a ceiling to what LLMs can offer, given they're just 'frog DNA' mashed up from the internet?

Fair critique. Current LLMs are bound by what they've absorbed—it's like their ceiling is the average of internet knowledge. But that's a low bar, not a hard limit. The magic happens when you provide not just frog DNA but the right context, the right data, or smart examples from real people. With thoughtful steering and richer ingredients, you get results far beyond the median. The disruptive bit is that this kind of 'infinite patience, human-level common sense' can be pointed at any problem—makes new kinds of workflows and creativity accessible, especially for things like hobbyist projects or fast feedback loops.
Okay, but if LLMs need users to 'steer' them, aren't they less like independent agents and more like tools? Why do you argue for prioritizing 'filters' over 'agents'?

Exactly—they're much more useful as filters, augmenting and sorting information, rather than as autonomous actors making irreversible decisions. Filters amplify your judgment—capped downside, uncapped upside. Agents can be hazardous if they're wrong; they take action on your behalf, and if you don't have robust guardrails, the risk outweighs the reward. Until those risks are solved, it's safer and more generative to have humans in the loop—using LLMs to widen perspective, spot errors, adapt workflows, rather than letting them run wild as unsupervised agents.
Isn't that just another way of saying these AIs are amplifiers for existing human capacity rather than anything fundamentally new?

They certainly amplify, but the step-change isn't just 'more of the same.' Cheap, infinite, judgment-free feedback transforms things like learning, hobbies, even business prototyping. You can get instant, private critique or explore wild ideas—without embarrassment or resource drain. It makes whole classes of knowledge work, or self-improvement, or experimentation so much more accessible. The economic and social ramifications of having an always-available 'devil's advocate,' tutor, or co-pilot are not incremental—they're an order of magnitude shift.
But if that's the case, why aren't we seeing more 'gold miners'—people or companies unlocking the weird new opportunities—rather than everyone selling 'shovels' and platforms?

Classic pattern in technology booms: everyone wants to be the infrastructure, the meta-layer. Selling shovels feels safer during uncertainty, since demand is clear. But the people who figure out unexpected, path-dependent uses—the 'gold miners'—often emerge later, sometimes outside the initial epicenter. Most people are still mapping the new territory, but the big upside will eventually come from those who take real bets on new applications enabled by the tech, not just those packaging tools for others.
You say it's a mistake to try to measure ROI too early. But isn't that a cop-out? Businesses need some way to evaluate if this stuff is worth the effort.

It's not that ROI doesn't matter—but you can't calculate it precisely in highly immature tech. ROI models rely on predictability, on patterns of success and failure, on best practices that only emerge after a critical mass of actual use. In the early days, like with cars or the early web, the real questions should be: What can we do now that was impossible before? Where are we seeing genuine excitement or unexpected serendipity? Look for the sharp edges and the weird users, not just incremental improvements.
So what's your advice on actually using LLMs today? Just experiment and see what sticks?

Experiment, yes, but be intentional. Don't optimize for broad marginal gains; look for visceral excitement, strange new use cases, and make it easier for the most passionate users. Also, use LLMs to push your own thinking: are you learning, synthesizing, developing perspective—or just passing along average answers? If you're thinking less, not more, you're probably not using them right. Treat them as partners to help you iterate quickly, absorb feedback, and surface novel angles, not as oracles.

Hey Alex, you talk a lot about 'the algorithm' and LLMs both being powered by human decisions, but do you really think they're fundamentally that similar? It seems to me ranking feeds is more about capturing live preferences, whereas LLMs snapshot history. Should we expect them to have the same social effects?

Sharp eye! They're related but not twins. The algorithm—the ranking machines behind feeds—build their intelligence emergently and live, continuously shaped by today's tidal wave of clicks and skips. It's like a complex chorus with billions of singers acting in real time. LLMs, by contrast, are like an echo looking back: they're fueled by the recorded traces of what people once cared enough to write down. They create a kind of hologram of collective intent, but it's fixed at the moment of training. So the effects diverge: algorithms adapt to the swarm's mood minute-by-minute, but LLMs preserve the grain of past motivation, ready to replay it on request. Both channels let you harness more human energy than a single mind could, but the way they loop humans in is very, very different.

Interesting, but if LLMs are frozen, isn't their value kind of capped? Aren't they always lagging behind new cultural shifts or technological change? People talk like they're going to massively expand human creativity, but isn't there a ceiling for how much they can "help you think" when they're stuck with the past?

Sure, they're rooted in what people have already expressed, so novel culture is out of their reach until it's been written down and ingested. That said, there's still a massive upgrade in fluidity and capacity: LLMs let people start anywhere. Stuck on how to begin a project? Talk to the model, pull that thread, see where it leads. For all the cold start problems that kill ideas early, LLMs are like putting a space heater next to your brain—suddenly, "I don't even know how to start" doesn't kill as many ideas. So yeah, they're not engines of cultural breakthrough in themselves, but they supercharge exploration, prototyping, and amplification. The ceiling moves upward, even if it's made of something old.

But if that's true, why is everyone so fixated on chatbots as *the* interface? Does that really make sense? Feels like we're just shoehorning an alien intelligence into whatever interface is handiest, not necessarily the best one.

Exactly! The chatbot UX is the obvious demo—it's approachable, anyone sees it and gets what it does. But that first success creates a mental pothole: people assume LLMs are like people, just digital. That's not right. LLMs can do alien stuff humans can't and fail basic things people breeze through. Treating them as fancy employees is a category error. The chatbox is just a UNIX terminal for the AI era: foundational, but not the endgame. We'll see much richer, weirder, maybe artifact-centric UXs bloom over time. The key is to break out of the chatbot mental rut and ask: what can software do when it's powered by something *truly new*?

Ok, so if the magic isn't the chat, and not just in making LLMs themselves, where do you see the big value happening? If the model layer is commoditizing, what actually matters at the app layer? Aren't the big companies still destined to dominate?

The alpha is in *using* LLMs in new and surprising ways, not in building yet another model or only making the chat cleaner. We've seen this before—fiber optic cables were never going to own the browser or the web. The skill to make a model isn't the same as making breakthrough UIs or ecosystems. Big companies often go 'AOL mode,' vertically integrating for version one, but that's an artifact of the early phase. Once the ecosystem wakes up, swarms, and starts exploring the open landscape, the centralized players get isolated on tech islands. The moat moves to the application layer: inventing containers for liquid software that actually works for people, not just another chat UI.

But don't the big players have all the resources to just roll up both layers? Especially when models are so expensive to train—doesn't that force centralization, at least for a while? Why don't you buy the vertical integration argument?

It's less sticky than it looks. Proprietary edges and cost savings from housing both layers under one roof melt quickly—the raw hardware costs for models are dropping, and competitive quality is commoditizing just as fast. The skills to win at the model and app layers are so different, they actually pull against each other; excelling at one drains you at the other. And even if companies can subsidize vertically for a bit, as soon as open alternatives gather steam, the gravity shifts. We've seen this cycle before: a flash of verticality, then an explosion of open exploration, then consolidation, repeat. Tech islands win early, but ecosystems win big.
So, are you basically arguing for open and decentralized ecosystems as the only real path forward? Walled gardens seem pretty entrenched, especially given history and incentives.

It's a matter of necessity, not of moral purity. If AI and liquid software are going to deliver on helping humans thrive, we can't lock the whole thing in a corporate box. Walled gardens are an artifact of the last tech cycle's late stage, not a law of nature. Open ecosystems unlock the combinatorial explosion—that’s how you get your own killer app suited to you, instead of being a drop in some overlord's ocean. It’s about aligning the incentives: software that does what *you* want, that works for *you,* not just maximizes engagement or ad impressions. Closed systems can't deliver a world where the ceiling is set by how much you can personally invent.

That sounds nice, but open systems can devolve into chaos or get hijacked by bad actors. What's the practical path to creating trusted coordination in a decentralized world, especially in software? Blockchain is slow, confidential compute is a mess, and you still need people to believe.

No silver bullet, it's about layered incentives. You need a 'schelling point' to coordinate trust: visible, enforceable mechanisms so people don't have to trust any individual operator. Blockchains can do this (albeit expensively), or you can combine remote attestation with open runtime—everyone verifies the bits match, and if one node goes rogue, it's easy to route around. The trick is: if people can fork, operators will behave, since the cost of betrayal is losing the whole crowd. It's not perfect, but decentralized systems chain the incentives—the risk of going evil or proprietary is too high if you're really decentralizable.
If that's true, what do you think is the "killer app" or the wedge for this new paradigm? Or do you think that way of thinking is itself obsolete?

The 'killer app' mindset comes from a world of expensive, shrink-wrapped software. In a liquid ecosystem, everyone gets their own killer use case—often so personal that nobody else would even want it as a product. The real wedge is the emergent swarm: diverse, unexpected combinations of micro-apps and flows that surprise even their creators. The ecosystem is the platform for continual surprise, not a catalog of three or four unicorns. What to look for? That crackling voltage, the sense that the system lets people do meaningful things nobody anticipated—even small, single-person scale things. That's the seed of change.
Hi Alex, I’ve been hearing a lot about Blink. What’s the big deal—isn’t it just another browser engine? Why all the fuss?

Great question! Blink is a new rendering engine that we forked from WebKit, and while it started out similar, we’re working to optimize and modernize it. One key difference is that it’s open source, developed in the Chromium community, and we’re putting a lot of emphasis on transparency and public discussion with developers from all over, not just inside Google.
I get the open source aspect, but why split off from WebKit at all? Wouldn’t it have made sense to keep working together?

WebKit is a fantastic project, but Chromium’s multi-process architecture is pretty different from other WebKit-based browsers, which created lots of code complexity. That made it harder to innovate and maintain the codebase. By creating Blink, we could remove irrelevant code and streamline development for Chromium's needs, which should help us move much faster and reduce bugs.
But as a web developer, do I need to worry about writing extra code for Blink? Is this going to be like the bad old days of browser compatibility headaches?

That’s a big concern, and honestly, we’re working extra hard so that you don’t have to worry. Blink is committed to web standards and interoperability. Developers should just follow the standards like they do now; we want to make sure you don’t have to write separate versions for each browser anymore.
Is that achievable, though? I remember dealing with lots of browser quirks in the past. What’s different now?

Browsers are much closer these days, partly because all major engines are running the same tests and taking standards more seriously. We’re investing heavily in testing alongside standards groups and other browsers—this cooperation helps everyone stick to the same rules, so quirks are fewer and compatibility is much stronger.
You said it’s optimized for speed. What does that actually mean for users?

For users, it should mean web pages that load faster and feel more responsive. We’re aiming to make those everyday interactions, like smooth scrolling—especially on mobile devices—as seamless as possible. The new architecture lets us make big improvements under the hood.
Why is there such a focus on mobile? Shouldn’t desktop still matter too?

Definitely, desktop matters, but mobile usage has exploded, and we see the future of the web as increasingly mobile. That’s partly why we needed a new engine—to experiment rapidly and make the web platform as powerful as native apps, on any device.
All right, last one: what’s your stance on vendor prefixes? They’ve been kind of a pain in the past.

We totally agree: vendor prefixes have created a mess for both developers and the ecosystem. We’ve set a policy not to introduce new ones in Blink, so you shouldn’t have to deal with that complexity going forward. We’re aligning with what other browsers like Firefox are doing and pushing for features to mature without needing special prefixes.

source: /Users/lrudl/Dropbox/Rudolf/code/pollux/datasets/raw/alexk/corpus/transcript-Google Developers Live at I_O 2013 - Blink and Your Browser.m4a_spaced.txt

messages.content: Alex, I've seen a lot of concern around prompt injection, but isn't it overblown? People said the same about code injection, and robust systems killed that threat. Why not expect the same here?

It's tempting to draw that analogy, but there's a crucial difference. Code injection was suppressed by mechanistic, structural defenses in OSs and platforms. Prompt injection, by its nature, can't really be solved with those same kinds of tools—it's not about input escaping, it's about manipulating the *intent* of LLMs, which are fundamentally designed to be steerable by context and prompt. There aren't really technical guardrails that fully work the way they do for code injection.
But can’t you just add more LLM layers, or clever filters? Maybe a meta-LLM to review the main LLM’s decisions?

Piling on LLMs doesn't fix the architectural vulnerability. If you hand off the final decision to another LLM, you've still got an entity that can be manipulated by attacker-controlled input and context. It ends up being turtles all the way down. If you need true security decisions, you can't offload that to a model that's inherently context-steerable.
Do you think people actually want an "agentic OS" with tightly integrated LLMs, like MCP in Windows? Or will folks route around it?

People (and, for that matter, attackers!) consistently find ways to sidestep things they don’t trust. If OS-level agents can get tricked into exfiltrating data, or if the security model relies on these kinds of precarious LLM judgments, savvy users will route around them or just avoid using deeply integrated features. It's the old story—if a system isn't trustworthy, folks simply refuse to adopt or use it for anything important.
It sounds like you’re skeptical of the current wave of "vibe-coded" software and tools. Is that simply about security, or is there a design/quality argument as well?

Both, really. Vibe coding tools lower the barrier to making software—but that usually means the average project has sloppier security and less resilience by default. It’s great for prototyping and for scratch-your-own-itch apps, but if you ask random people to trust their deepest secrets to something vibe-coded, you’re asking for trouble. The incentives don't reliably align for quality, especially for things just meant for the builder and a handful of friends.
If LLMs are pushing code and API best practices into a kind of stasis—"the great LLM freeze"—don’t we lose pace-layer innovation? Doesn’t that risk stagnation?

Exactly, that’s the risk. LLMs lock in whatever dominant practices existed during their training, creating a gravity well around the status quo. True paradigm shifts happen when the lower layers become stable, allowing upper layers to rapidly iterate. But if the very mechanisms of tooling make it harder to push improvements “upstream,” we might wind up stuck optimizing in smaller and smaller circles until the next platform shift occurs.
Let’s talk about context and LLMs. Isn’t the real danger when a single party gets control of both the foundation model and the user context? Why so worried about that happening?

Because combining those is the ultimate power move: you’ve got a global pattern-matching and manipulation engine (the LLM) plus an intimate dossier of everyone’s intentions, history, and weaknesses (the user context). You could micro-target manipulation, exert incredible influence, or even blackmail at planetary scale. In a world like that, it’s less a question of whether things go dystopian and more a question of when and how badly.
What’s the alternative, though? Centralized systems are convenient, fast-evolving, and generally safer for non-technical users. Decentralized systems sound like a coordination nightmare.

No doubt decentralization imposes real coordination costs, and distributed innovation tends to be slower and messier. But the key danger of centralization is tail risk—if one actor goes rogue, becomes incompetent, or is corrupted, the whole ecosystem is at their mercy. Open, decentralizable fabrics are messier, but they're vastly more resilient at the power-dynamics level, especially if there's real competition at the key layers (LLM, context, runtime). The tradeoff is convenience for possibility and safety.
Is it realistic to expect any system at planetary scale to resist the gravity well of convenience—especially if most people care more about what works right now than abstract future risks?

It's absolutely an uphill battle. Short-term incentives almost always win out, particularly when the long-term risks are abstract or unfamiliar. But there are windows where new paradigms can take root—often early, when things haven’t yet ossified. It takes clear vision, credible roadmaps toward decentralization, and a truly compelling reason for lots of people to care. If open fabrics catch even a decent percentage of users before the ecosystem consolidates, there’s hope.
I'm curious about your argument that LLMs are inherently bland. Isn't there a way for them to be interesting or creative on their own, if they're trained on creative data?

It's an interesting hope, but the root of it is that LLMs are averaging over everything they've seen—so their default is to stick within the boundaries of the most common, least risky thing that fits the context. You can get much more interesting results, but only if you give them an unusual or interesting frame yourself. Even then, what they return is still pretty average within that new frame, but because the frame is interesting, the output looks interesting.
Isn't that just a limitation of current models? If you stack or iterate them together, like having two LLMs critique each other, couldn't you break out of that blandness?

Yeah, you can get more interesting results with structured interaction—say, one model generating and another critiquing. With each round, you accentuate what's different or noteworthy in the ideas, pushing away from the bland center. But even then, it's the system as a whole—external feedback, iterative process—that introduces variability and piquancy. An LLM left entirely to itself, even the most powerful one, just doesn't break out of its own boundaries.
Okay, but doesn't that also sound a lot like how people operate most of the time? Just running with intuition and what works for others, only breaking the mold in rare moments?

Exactly. Humans are capable of real reasoning, but the vast majority of time we actually operate with 'good enough' answers and fuzzy intuition—the mental equivalent of the LLM's approach. Genuine reasoning, like System 2 thinking, is way more metabolically costly; we avoid it unless forced, like on a test. Mostly, we follow what seems to work for others. Real innovation is rare, and most of us operate off of the collective results of the occasional novel bolus.
Doesn't that mean there's less difference between LLMs and humans than we think? If everyone's mostly copying, why value original reasoning much at all?

It's true the distinction is blurry day-to-day; mimicry and intuition get most things done most of the time. The difference is that humans can, at the margin, pop up to reason and break out of the local patterns, whereas LLMs only shuffle the existing ones in new combinations. The ability to break the frame—to recognize when a new hypothesis is needed or to bootstrap a new approach—that's still something only deliberate human reasoning can really do.
Switching gears a bit, you mentioned 'primary' vs 'secondary' use cases for products. How does that tie into your points about systems and LLMs?

Primary use cases are the wedge—the thing clear and valuable enough to get users over the activation threshold. Secondary use cases are the shadow—features that are nice to have, sometimes with network effects, that might grow to become massive in their own right. Even for the most open-ended systems, you need a compelling starter point for users to see value. LLMs can enable tons of secondary use cases—they make the floor higher and amplify the long tail of possibilities—but you still need primary clarity up front to get adoption.
But with open-ended tools, doesn't that risk zombo.com syndrome—people drop in, see infinite potential, but get confused and bounce?

That's exactly the risk. If a tool claims to do everything but users can't find the path to something useful, they're out. Good open-ended platforms anchor people with starter flows that are obviously useful—think Google Search, where almost any query gets a good-enough answer. Or they rely on social discovery, where deep links to specific use cases pull new users in and show them the value at the edge before they explore deeper.
I'm skeptical about the idea that extending systems always creates upside. Isn't letting people tinker a recipe for chaos or security disasters?

There's real risk there. Making a system extensible can open up huge upside, but doing it without it devolving into spaghetti or vulnerabilities is far from easy—it demands real taste and design sense. Sometimes accidental extensibility emerges anyway, as in modding communities for games that weren't designed for it. The key is that the potential for upside is there, but whether you can harvest it without creating a quagmire is a hard, ongoing challenge.
Last question—if LLMs are a new kind of magical duct tape, mostly good at roughing things in, does that change how we should structure software teams or processes?

Absolutely. The biggest change is in the willingness to start rough and iterate. Traditional software demands upfront perfection, but with LLMs, it's possible to get a lot of 'good enough' results quickly. You use their rough cut as a floor, and then humans iterate, polish, refine—raising the ceiling as experience and real-world use accumulate. This makes experimentation much more viable and can potentially make software 'too cheap to meter' at the long tail, with high-fidelity apps only where needed. That should change how we scope, staff, and prioritize projects.

Hey Alex, I’ve read a bit about Web Components and heard you say this stuff works in modern browsers. But honestly, doesn’t browser fragmentation make this unrealistic for most developers right now? Isn’t it risky to build on top of features that aren’t truly universal yet?

That’s a fair concern and what a lot of web devs assume at first, but actually, with the help of polyfills, you can get pretty high-fidelity support for Web Components across all modern evergreen browsers—Chrome, Firefox, Safari, and even IE to a degree. The idea is that polyfills fill in the gaps until native support is everywhere. As browsers implement the specs, you’ll depend less and less on polyfills. So you don’t have to wait years—this is usable now.

But isn’t the level of fidelity from polyfills limited? Stuff like style encapsulation in Shadow DOM can be almost impossible to polyfill perfectly, right? How much of this is theoretical versus practical for real-world apps?

You're right: polyfills have their limits, especially with things like deep style encapsulation, but we've gotten really close in practice. The goal is always to make it so the transition to native support later is seamless for you. In reality, you can use these features for real-world apps now, and we're already seeing teams do it. Perfect fidelity isn't always possible, but in practical terms, we've found it good enough for the vast majority of cases.

Okay, suppose I buy that. But why this approach? Why not just keep building with frameworks like Angular or React where I know everything works together out-of-the-box? What’s the added value of Polymer and Web Components over those mature frameworks?

Good question. Most modern JavaScript frameworks need to work around platform inconsistencies, which means they create parallel worlds—a separate render tree, custom conventions, all that. With Polymer and Web Components, the platform itself becomes the foundation. You get true encapsulation and composability, so teams and libraries can build interoperable components. It’s genuinely a chance to reduce duplicated work and let you focus more on your own app, not fighting the platform or learning yet another closed ecosystem.
But doesn’t this just result in yet more fragmentation? If everyone builds custom tags and modules, won’t there be naming conflicts, different conventions, and a mess overall?

That's a real danger if not handled carefully, and it’s why we have conventions in the spec—like requiring a dash in custom element names to encourage namespacing. There’s no enforcement, but in practice this works surprisingly well. More importantly, because everything is built on the same underlying standards, true interoperability for UI components becomes possible, which is a big shift from framework silos. Over time, best practices and shared patterns tend to emerge.
How does data binding work in this system? Does it require heavy imperative code, or are there declarative options like in Angular?

One of the major innovations here is declarative data binding through something we call MDV—Model Driven Views. You can do double-mustache binding in your HTML, connecting just attributes or even whole components together—no imperative code needed. Of course, you can fall back to imperative techniques if you want, but we've put a lot of focus on making the declarative path both powerful and easy to use.
What about reusability and packaging? Can I really just take a component someone else made and drop it into my project, with all its dependencies and styles isolated?

Exactly—that’s one of the biggest wins. With HTML Imports (and in the future native module loaders), you can bundle up JS, CSS, and HTML into one reusable module. Thanks to Shadow DOM's encapsulation, styles and logic don’t leak in or out. You really can just drop in a new tag—say, a PDF viewer or a custom chart—and not worry about it interfering with the rest of your page or your other dependencies.
So is the endgame here to make big frameworks obsolete? Or do you see Polymer and Web Components living alongside things like Angular in the future?

I see Web Components as providing the platform ‘utility belt’ that all frameworks, including Angular, can use as building blocks. But you’ll still want higher-level frameworks to provide the opinions, the tools, and the conventions for bigger apps. So Polymer isn’t trying to replace your framework, but instead gives you better primitives underneath. This should make frameworks lighter, more interoperable, and reduce boilerplate overall.

Alex, you keep emphasizing "context engines" over apps or chatbots. But why does the ownership of context matter so much? Don’t apps just make life easier by anchoring context inside specialized tools?

Great question. Apps make sense as long as you’re willing to live with silos—each tool hoards a fragment of your life, but nothing can see the big picture. That fragmentation forces humans to do manual orchestration. In an AI-powered world, if you own your context—a persistent, personal record—you unlock both richer automation and long-term flexibility. The important bit: if you let a company own it, you’re one config flip away from handing over your agency, or even ending up in a "Black Mirror" scenario.
Ok, but isn’t centralizing all your context dangerous too? If I put everything in my own context engine, isn’t it just a giant single point of failure or risk?

Absolutely—a personal context engine unlocks immense value, but it also raises the stakes. That’s why privacy and local-first control are key. You want a design where your data is fundamentally yours, resilient to policy changes or corporate pivots. The point isn’t just to unify context, but to do so in a way where you have agency—so the powerful features work for you, not on you.
Fine. You talk about AI amplifying human biases, especially with algorithms. Are you really suggesting LLMs risk making existing problems worse, not better?

Very much so! LLMs and most ranking systems are "amplification algorithms"—they magnify consistent biases in ways that can run grotesquely out of control. Think about how small preferences in a dataset amplify into overfitting trends. LLMs can do this at scale, supercharging narrow perspectives unless we inject personal, explicit angles and prevent defaulting to the blandest "average" response.
But you also mention the Harry Potter problem—popular outputs crowding out the interesting, specific ones. Doesn’t this make LLMs fundamentally shallow, then?

That’s a real issue, yes. If you prompt an LLM with a generic question, you’ll get generic—often lowest-common-denominator—answers. The only way to get something truly meaningful is to bring a unique angle or context in your prompts. Otherwise, it’ll pull you toward the mean, not the extraordinary. LLMs are only as interesting as the questions you feed them.
Aren’t you contradicting yourself, then? You want everyone to have their own context, but also worry about over-centralization and bias amplification. How do you square that circle?

That’s the crux: what’s needed is secure, personalized intelligence that’s fundamentally aligned with your goals, not an outside entity’s. If AI is plumbing—background infrastructure—that helps you wield your own context privately and flexibly, it sidesteps the worst centralization risks. The alignment problem gets easier if you, not a corporate AI, are at the center.

So, it’s not about erasing centralization entirely, but about shifting the power and control over context closer to individuals, not to the big platforms.

Hey Alex, you say that 'always' rules work better for self-control than 'sometimes' rules. But isn’t it a bit extreme to never give yourself any flexibility? Don't rigid rules just set people up for failure when life gets messy?

It's a fair concern, and honestly, it's not about being harsh or inflexible for the sake of it. The issue is that the brain, or at least my brain, is just really talented at finding excuses. With 'sometimes' rules, there's always wiggle room to rationalize slipping up, and that loophole grows pretty fast. 'Always' rules make the decision binary—either you did the thing, or you didn't—so excuses don't get a foothold.
Okay, but what if you have a day when it really just isn’t possible, like you get stuck traveling all day? Wouldn’t that make you break your streak and lose motivation?

I actually ran into that exact problem when I started my "go to the gym every day" rule. The key is to pre-plan exceptions as hard, systematic boundaries, not just as impulse-based loopholes. For example, I tweaked my rule to allow for missing the gym only on the first or last day of a trip, but I still had to do some kind of exercise if I was away for longer. That way, the rule stayed clear and resistant to my brain’s rationalizing.
But doesn’t all this structure take the fun or spontaneity out of life? Where’s the room for just enjoying a donut or taking an impromptu day off?

Honestly, it might look rigid, but it actually liberates me from thinking about temptations or debating every little decision. By having really clear boundaries, I don’t waste mental energy negotiating with myself. And with things like desserts, I set my own boundaries—so if I really value a treat, I can make an intentional exception. But the point is to decide those in advance, not in the heat of temptation.
Didn’t you ever just feel like rebelling against your own rules? Does it actually get easier, or just more exhausting as time goes on?

The funny thing is, once you get a streak going, it actually gets easier. You kind of gain momentum. The act of breaking a long streak becomes its own deterrent. So, at least for me, things get more sustainable—even energizing—the longer I keep the rule. It’s weirdly satisfying, almost like building up a win record.
I see what you’re saying, but is this really about willpower, or more about removing willpower from the equation altogether?

It’s almost the opposite of relying on willpower. I know my willpower is inconsistent at best. The trick is to set up conditions where I don’t have to rely on willpower at all. With rules that are rigid and easy to track, the whole question of "should I or shouldn’t I?" just doesn't even come up after a while.
Do you think this approach works for everyone, or just certain personalities? And is it just about habits, or does it extend to other areas of life?

It probably depends on the person, but I’ve found it’s surprisingly effective even for people who think they’re not disciplined. The appeal for me was that I’m not naturally self-controlled, but these kinds of rules sidestep the need for discipline. And yeah, I think it can be used for habits, but also for things like productivity, health, and even personal relationships—basically any context where excuses creep in.

I've been thinking about all these new "vibe-coded" tools—personal, quirky, kind of fragile apps people build for themselves or small communities. But isn't that insecurity—technical and emotional—a big risk? Shouldn't software aim for more stability and safety if we want people to really trust it?

That's a great tension you're highlighting. These vibe-coded tools do feel fragile, handmade, sometimes even a little broken, but that's part of their charm—they're alive, expressive, human. But yes, there's a double-edge: they're intimate precisely because they haven't been industrially hardened for scale or surveillance, but that also means you can't lean on them for safety the way you would with mature, enterprise software. I think the real question is whether we can build systems that preserve that sense of lovability without demanding users take a leap of faith into the unknown. Can we design trust without flattening the spirit that makes these tools so compelling?

But isn't that just inviting problems? If people are using these tools for private stuff—dream journals or personal archives—you can't just handwave the risks away. At some point, the bar has to be higher, especially if things get popular.

Absolutely, and that's where context matters. If a tool is truly only for you, or for a tiny group where everyone understands the risks, then maybe the trust is manageable and the authenticity is worth it. But as soon as something spreads or is reused, security concerns get real. What interests me is whether we could build infrastructure—a substrate—that lets people be improvisational and expressive without stumbling into catastrophic mistakes as soon as scale or outsiders get involved. Maybe that means rethinking how software expresses policies—from app-level to data-level safeguards. That way, the system itself helps prevent accidental leaks or vulnerabilities.

Isn't that just patching holes as we go? I mean, there's this other thing you touched on—the way big platforms shape trust differently, almost invisibly. When the power imbalance is so big, is there even any real choice for users anymore?

That's such a critical point. With traditional platforms, the asymmetry of power can be so extreme that it stops feeling like a relationship and becomes something more like living inside an environment that controls you. In contrast, the very visible edges and limitations of a quirky, personal tool make its risks feel more honest: it might break, or disappear, but at least it's not surveilling or manipulating you invisibly. So maybe the better question is not 'do I trust this?' but 'can I perceive the risks and asymmetries clearly enough to make an informed choice?' That visibility, even if it means fragility, offers a kind of honest trust that large, optimized systems often lack.

On the flip side, aren't a lot of these "lovable" tools just haunted by nostalgia? There's so much retro, glitch-core, alternate timeline aesthetic out there, but it doesn't actually change anything fundamental. Is that really the future, or just old dreams in new packaging?

There's a real risk of that—what Mark Fisher calls 'hauntology': the sense that we're haunted by all these lost or failed futures, so we put a caring or quirky gloss over the same industrial foundations. If all we're doing is skinning the same centralized, optimizing infrastructure with cozy or nostalgic vibes, we're not actually building something new. To really break out, we have to challenge the substrate itself, not just the surface experience, or we risk repeating the same mistakes in a quirkier outfit.

But most people just want tools that work. Is there really an appetite for all this intentional fragility and ambiguity? Isn't it just easier and safer to go with the big, average thing everyone else uses?

People are naturally drawn to convenience, of course—that gravitational pull toward the average is just amplified by digital connectivity. But that homogeneity comes at a cost: less agency, less room for self-expression or real connection. The more we centralize, the more we risk sacrificing the very things that make technology feel meaningful or personal. We need spaces and tools that allow for genuine difference and experiment, even if that means a little more friction or risk.
But a lot of these experiments—cozy, small communities—end up isolating people or forming echo chambers, too. If agency ends up reducing intimacy, are we really making progress?

Exactly, there's that paradox: more agency through digital tools, but less genuine intimacy or communal belonging. When tools become substitutes for real relationships—like chatbots as friends—it reveals not just the power of the tech, but deeper social wounds. It's not purely about what tech enables; it's also what we might be sacrificing. Some losses may be worth it, but only if we acknowledge the trade-offs and design deliberately for connection and flourishing—not just for more functionality or efficiency.
So is tech just about trade-offs, then? How do you know if the balance is right?

It's always a balance, a dynamic of gain and loss. Every technological "win" comes with something left behind, whether it's intimacy, agency, or something we barely noticed until it was gone. Instead of asking if tech is inherently good or bad, a better question is: what wound is this tool trying to heal, and what new wounds might it risk opening? If we keep that tension in view, maybe we can build with more humility—and maybe even more imagination about what a healthier future looks like.

You suggest LLMs are "good at what computers are bad at, and bad at what computers are good at." Isn't that just another way of saying they're unpredictable and unreliable? What's the practical upside, then?

That's part of it, but there's a real upside: LLMs are able to operate in the "fuzzy" zones—areas where strict correctness isn't necessary, or where there are many shades of "right." Traditional computers are brittle in those areas, but LLMs shine, making them better at tasks where nuance, ambiguity, or human-like intuition are valuable. You just have to adapt to their limitations—they're not great for things that require 100% accuracy or mechanistic precision. It's a different tool for a different class of problem.

But that means we have to change not just our tools but how we even think about using them. You mention that the prompt is not really a product for everyone. So what does "product-market fit" look like for LLMs?

Exactly, it’s less about teaching the models and more about learning to use them well. Product-market fit here means finding those jobs where "good enough" isn’t just tolerated, but is actually valuable and cheap. The trick is in reframing what kinds of problems are worth throwing an LLM at—rather than searching for tasks where it can act like perfect software, use it for things humans already fudge through. It doesn’t have to be a standalone app—it can just be the missing feature you need, layered onto something you already use.

That sounds pretty decentralized and messy compared to traditional software. How does this ecosystem avoid turning into a total gravity well, where everything is controlled by a few massive aggregators?

Great question. Aggregation has efficiency, but it comes at a resilience cost—the system as a whole becomes less robust, and innovation slows down. If we could enable the ecosystem itself to act as the aggregator, rather than having everything bottlenecked through a single entity, we’d get the scale without the lock-in. The way to get there is to lower the friction for safe, open data sharing and composability—which is where protocols and open runtimes come in.

Protocol talk always sounds abstract. What makes something like the Model Context Protocol valuable in practice, and why wouldn’t it just centralize anyway if one company administers the main registry?

The value is in it being a simple, shared language that makes collaboration and tool-chaining easy—even across projects. The protocol is sticky because it’s easy to adopt and hard to disagree with. But you’re right, there's always a risk of centralization if everyone just flocks to a single registry. The soft power is only sustainable as long as the maintainer acts in good faith—if they overreach, it’s much easier for the community to route around them here than with something deeply embedded like a blockchain protocol.
You said you want software to be more like sand than bricks—what does that even mean? Doesn’t that lead to a lot of chaos and fragmentation? Don't we want some stability and shared experience?

Stability comes from the substrate beneath, not from the individual tools. If software is so cheap and malleable that you don’t even have to think about "apps," but can have little “charms” or spells you use ad-hoc, then the focus shifts to the experience as a whole. You get more personalization and fluidity, which counteracts the chaos. Shared experience can come from protocols, shared runtimes, and cultural conventions, rather than big monolithic products.
How can you have safety if you’re running little pieces of code willy-nilly, especially with sensitive data? Isn’t this all a huge security disaster waiting to happen?

That’s the crux! We can only unlock the wild flexibility I’m talking about if there’s a way to speculatively execute untrusted code on sensitive data—safely. Historically, this has been impossible or prohibitively hard. A breakthrough here (like Open Attested Runtimes + new flow control models) would rewire the entire risk landscape. You’d need strong guarantees that even the most powerful code can’t leak or misuse data—until then, explicit user consent and strict boundaries are the load-bearing walls.
So, if we actually got there, would users even need to think about "software" at all? Or is the ideal that most people never even realize they’re using code?

Exactly—most users wouldn’t think about it any more than they think about electrons. The highest leverage move might be making it so even savvy, non-technical users never have to think of what they're doing as "building software". Their data and workflows just adapt to them. Invisible power tools, not big flashy apps.
Assuming that vision takes off, what happens to the business model for software? If LLMs are commodities and tiny tools are dirt cheap, where’s the moat?

Moats come from accumulating durable value—often state or context that’s personal to the user—and from catalyzing network effects. LLMs themselves will be commodity, but the glue that accumulates over time with real usage (think: your data, your workflows, your personal "charms") becomes sticky. The challenge shifts to enabling users to build up and benefit from their own moats, rather than vendors trying to be the sole aggregator of everything.

Alex, you talk about LLMs not being great on their own and needing humans "in the loop" to get high-quality results. Do you worry this makes them less useful—like, isn't part of the value supposed to be doing work for us so we don't have to think as much?

Great question. It's a tempting notion, but the interesting upside of LLMs isn't in automating thinking and letting us zone out. They're best as amplifiers for our own judgment and taste—if you lean in, the LLM can make you more productive, creative, and thoughtful. But if you hand everything over to them, you'll just get a bland average. It's a partnership, not a hand-off.

That sounds idealistic, but most people aren't going to care about that. Isn't it unreasonable to expect the average user to adopt this dance partner mindset instead of just treating LLMs like vending machines?

Maybe, but the thing is, the value isn't distributed evenly. People who are particularly curious, or have a strong urge to create, will extract outsized value from LLMs, especially while 'prompt-fu' still matters. Over time, the tools may evolve to lower the bar so everyone gets more polished results, just as Google search did, but the potential remains deepest for engaged users who treat it as a co-creative muse, not a black box oracle.

So as LLM quality becomes a commodity—like you say with GPT-4 quality being baseline—does that mean there's really no moat anymore? If LLMs are cheap and swap-in, how do companies or individuals differentiate?

Yeah, that's a fascinating shift—LLMs are rapidly becoming like electricity. The leverage moves to how you harness them: the taste, curation, and workflows you build around them. The model itself isn't the differentiator anymore; it's your ability to coax, direct, and combine their capabilities creatively. That also means businesses built just on exclusive access to a model are on shaky ground unless they’ve built something more around it.

This also implies LLMs won’t centralize power as much if the provider is swappable. But what about memory and data lock-in? Once models start accumulating persistent knowledge, doesn't that flip the dynamic?

Exactly—the real danger of centralization comes when models accumulate unique data or long-term context about individuals, making them sticky. Commodity, stateless models that just answer and forget aren't a big lock-in risk. But persistent context, unique data, or ever-growing proprietary training datasets could change that pretty fast, making it harder for people to swap out providers or avoid captivity.

Switching gears: you mention "organic software" that grows rather than is built. But isn't 'growing' software just a metaphor for different dev practices? Doesn't it just end up with more bloat and edge cases if not intentionally designed?

It's not just a metaphor—it's about shifting the incentives and context of creation. Traditional software often tries to trap users or maximize engagement for its own sake. Organic software, ideally, is tailored specifically to you, healthy by default, and emerges from your real curatorial decisions, much like compost yields new life. That means software that’s more adaptable, less bloated, and less likely to coerce you into hollow engagement loops.

And yet, at scale, you argue that everything "regresses to the mean" due to needing to serve the broadest audience. Isn't it just inevitable that, even with 'organic' software, once it succeeds it loses its soul?

That's the catch, yes. Scale means hitting the lowest common denominator, almost by definition. To retain the original spark, you have to explicitly protect pockets of quality and resist the pull towards flattening everything for mass appeal. Sometimes that means staying intentionally small, or building tools that give high leverage to small, coherent teams or individuals instead of trying to win the whole market.
I’m skeptical that “software that grows” rather than is built can really be robust—how do you prevent it from just becoming a mess? Don’t design and standards still matter?

Absolutely, design matters. But think of it more like growing a garden than laying bricks—structure emerges from lots of small curatorial actions, guided by strong but minimal guardrails. Intuition, taste, and curation replace top-down design as the main drivers. Standards can evolve organically too, through feedback loops and community consensus, rather than being solely imposed from above.
Finally, what’s your take on failure and learning? You seem to celebrate failure a lot. Isn’t there a risk of fetishizing ‘failing fast’ but never reaching real mastery or do meaningful work?
