Failing isn’t the end; it’s the only way to learn anything worth knowing, as long as the feedback is fast and the cost of mistakes is survivable. Mastery emerges when practice is easy and you can push beyond your limits without it being a slog. The goal isn’t to chase failure blindly, but to iterate quickly, seek disconfirming evidence, and build confidence and resilience along the way. Real greatness comes from embracing the cycle, not avoiding mistakes.
You mention the web was initially revolutionary for its low friction. But clearly that didn't carry over to mobile, so what exactly derailed the web's dominance on mobile?

That's a great point. The web was indeed built on desktop assumptions—plenty of power, always-on fast internet, and no need to worry about battery. When mobile arrived, suddenly you had limited CPU, battery constraints, and intermittent connectivity. The result was that web experiences felt clunky by comparison, especially versus native apps that could take full advantage of the device. The decisive factor was apps simply provided a richer, more reliable experience in that environment. Over time, users just gravitated to whatever was smoother and more engaging.
So if native apps work so well for your most engaged users, why not just double down on that channel? Why bother with the web at all if engagement seems so lopsided?

Doubling down on native apps covers only a subset of your possible user base: the superfans. But the real missed opportunity is all the potential users who don’t want to install an app. App installation is a heavy commitment for most people—it takes space, time, and precious home screen real estate. Most businesses, especially those with infrequent-use cases, just can't get a meaningful share of users to install and keep their app. That leaves a massive gap where you’re simply not engaging huge swaths of potential customers.
But haven't mobile websites traditionally underperformed too? Isn't 'just use the mobile web' a bit naive given how bad those experiences tend to be?

You’re not wrong. A lot of businesses have historically just squished their desktop web presence down and called it mobile. That's not enough. The key to better engagement is to rethink from the ground up—what does the user actually want to accomplish when they're on their phone, often just killing time or seeking inspiration? If you tailor the experience for mobile contexts—quick, immersive, valuable interactions—you can absolutely capture more value and engagement, but it requires a real investment, not just a resize job.
What makes it technically hard for web experiences on mobile to match apps? Is it just about design thinking, or are there real platform limitations?

It's both. Design thinking is crucial, but historically there have also been hard technical barriers: unreliable connectivity, lack of system-level features like push notifications, and the pain of typing or re-engaging on mobile. Apps had native access, local resources, and could re-engage users with notifications. The web couldn't do those for a long time—but that's now changing with new web standards being widely adopted.
But hasn't the web always struggled with offline or flaky connectivity? How's that gap being closed?

Right, and poor or lost connectivity has been a real Achilles' heel for web experiences. The solution is new standards—service workers, background sync, and local caching—allowing sites to preload content, work offline, or pick up seamlessly as connectivity comes and goes. So you no longer have to hit that 'dinosaur' error screen; valuable experiences can persist even without a live connection.
You mention push notifications—aren't those just spammy distractions? Why are they essential for closing the gap with apps?

Push notifications got a bad rap for overuse, but used thoughtfully they’re just a way to give timely, relevant updates that keep users engaged, especially given how fleeting attention is on mobile. For instance, letting a user know a back-in-stock item is now available, or there’s breaking news—we tend to underappreciate how powerful controlled, opt-in re-engagement can be. Apps set the expectation here, so for the web to match, it needs to offer the same capabilities.
Are there any real success stories? Or is this just a theoretical improvement until more businesses actually ship great mobile web experiences?

We're seeing a lot of real-world success—retailers, media properties, service companies have launched progressive web apps and published impressive results. Increased engagement, more time spent, higher conversion rates. Companies like Alibaba, Twitter, Forbes, West Elm—across verticals and geographies—have shown this isn’t just theory. The numbers speak for themselves when the investment is made.
Last question: Is this really a paradigm shift, or just catch-up for the web platform? What’s fundamentally new?

It really is a paradigm shift. Like with "Ajax" a decade ago, we’re not just playing catch-up, we’re unlocking new expectations for interactivity, reliability, and engagement on the web. Progressive web apps mean businesses can reach more users without compromising on experience, offering app-like capabilities but with web-like reach and frictionless access. It’s about bridging the best of both worlds and being thoughtful, ambitious, and user-centered in how we build for modern mobile usage.

You talk a lot about the 'gardener' metaphor for systems, especially in organizations. But for practical purposes, isn’t a builder approach just more efficient? Why not just plan, execute, and control as much as possible, especially if you need results fast?

Great question! The builder approach can feel more efficient in the short run, especially for problems with clear boundaries and predictable outcomes. But with complex systems—like organizations or markets—control is mostly an illusion. You can plan and push, but the most magical, resilient results come from tending to what's alive, nudging, and responding, not forcing. It’s really about recognizing where you have influence vs. control, and knowing that patient, indirect interventions can ultimately deliver bigger outcomes than heroics.
But doesn’t this take a lot longer? What if you don’t have the luxury of time, or if people in the company are demanding visible progress?

It does sometimes take longer to see visible results when you adopt a gardening mindset—but it also reduces risk. Organizations crave momentum, so sometimes it’s pragmatic to take the obvious next step, just to avoid paralysis. However, if everything is about fast, top-down progress, you miss the compounding effects and serendipity that come from nurturing acorns. The trick is to combine both: provide enough motion to avoid stasis, but be patient and opportunistic enough to spot and water the seeds that actually want to grow.
You mention 'farming miracles' and optimizing for serendipity. Isn’t this just code for luck, though? How do you know your efforts aren’t just random chance?

That’s the beauty and frustration of it—when you farm for miracles, it always looks like luck from the outside. But there’s actually a method: planting lots of cheap, diverse seeds (projects, ideas, talent) and paying attention to which ones catch wind, then putting energy behind them. The gardener isn’t just hoping; they’re constantly scanning, tending, and pruning. Over time, these compounding bets mean your organization is structurally more likely to benefit from positive surprises. But yes, you have to be okay with credit sometimes flowing elsewhere, and outcomes being mostly indirect.

Doesn’t this make it harder for organizations to recognize or reward the value you create? If the success is diffuse or attributed to luck, won’t people stick with the safer, more visible builder moves?

Absolutely. Performance systems in most orgs reward visible, attributable success, not indirect outcomes. That's why the Saruman archetype dominates: bold plans, clear credit. The Radagast-type value—catalyzing, tending, weaving connections—is harder to see and almost always dismissed as luck or irrelevant. It takes particular leaders, cultures, or personal conviction to do gardener work despite minimal visibility. But when you look at the deepest, most resilient successes—ecosystems, products with unlikely network effects—they usually trace back to gardening, not blueprinting.
Isn’t this all a bit fatalistic? If everything is so complex and emergent, isn’t it tempting to just shrug and say nothing can really be changed?

It can feel nihilistic at first, yes—complexity is overwhelming! But systems thinking isn’t about resigning; it’s about recognizing constraints, then iterating within your adjacent possible. Even if your move set is small today, every action reshapes the possibilities. Over time, deliberate iterations can arc you somewhere you never imagined at first. The point isn’t hopelessness—it’s humility and strategic optimism.
Can you get more concrete? What’s an example of a miracle you farmed in a real organization?

Sure. One example: a division’s strategy was obviously failing, but nobody could say so out loud. Rather than confronting it head-on (which would go nowhere), I mentored a respected PM to articulate a coherent alternative—just enough, in the right format, at the right time. When a big reorg happened, the new leader seized this acorn as the new North Star. On the surface, it looked lucky, but it was the intentional tending of an idea, waiting for the right moment for it to grow.
How do you balance going with the flow versus sticking to a vision or plan? Don’t you risk drifting if you’re too opportunistic?

Great systems work combines an approximate North Star—a plausible, energizing future state everyone could believe in—with ruthless iteration through small, viable next steps. The North Star keeps you off local maxima; the gardening ensures you don’t waste energy on non-viable paths. If you just iterate, you might corner yourself. If you just plan, you chase grandiosity at the cost of reality. The tension is healthy, and you want both.
